topic: A neighbor wants to install a fence
raw_output:
[story 1]

The afternoon sun felt too bright, a physical pressure against my eyelids. I stared at the freshly mown lawn, the vibrant green blurring into an indistinct wash of color. Next door, the rhythmic *thwack* of a hammer echoed, sharp and insistent. Mr. Henderson was out there, all purposeful strides and the clatter of tools. My feet felt heavy, rooted to the patchy grass as I watched him wrestle with the posts. I tried to focus on the chipped paint of my windowsill, but the edges of the room seemed to shimmer, shifting in and out of focus. "Hello?" I managed to croak, the sound thin and reedy. He paused, wiping sweat from his brow, and looked over, his expression a mixture of mild annoyance and expectation.

[story 2]

The air in the hallway tasted metallic, like old pennies. Every sound seemed amplified—the distant siren, the hum of the refrigerator—all pressing in on my eardrums. I kept tracing patterns on the wallpaper, the floral design warping under my touch. Outside, the conversation was a low murmur, punctuated by the grating sound of metal against wood. The neighbor, Mr. Peterson, was gesturing emphatically toward the property line, his voice booming with an unshakeable conviction. I blinked several times, trying to reorient myself to the solid reality of the apartment, but the floor seemed to tilt slightly beneath my worn slippers. I just stood there, breathing shallowly, watching the boundary line being staked out.

[story 3]

I couldn't recall what I had been doing just moments ago. One second I was making tea, the next I was standing by the window, watching the neighbor’s activity. The world felt layered, like looking through thick, uneven glass. The sound of sawing wood was a relentless, high-pitched whine that burrowed into my skull. When the man finally stopped to adjust a length of wire, his gaze swept over me—a quick, assessing glance that didn't quite land. My mouth felt dry, and I found myself gripping the edge of the curtain so tightly my knuckles turned white. I wanted to ask a question, any question, but the words caught somewhere behind my ribs, leaving only a strange, hollow ache.
